title: practical english usage - Part 31
content:
Have you heard about Daniel and Corinne?

a  as an auxiliary verb, to make perfect verb forms (►  22) Have is used in several different ways: 21 have:     introducti      on  _________________ There was this man, see, and he couldn 't get up in the mornings. So he . ..

this has an indefinite sense.

Another apparent exception is in oral stories that begin There was this..., when 'Where can he sleep? 'Well, there's always the attic.' 'Who could we ask? 'Well, there's James, or Miranda, or Anna, or Emma, ..

Be,  have      and   do            2 grammar •  23   have: actions not I have lunch at 12.30 most days, (     I've luneh .. .) I'm having a bad day.

Did you have a good time (     Hadyethargoedrtime?) are possible. Contractions and weak forms of have are not used.

In this structure, we  make questions and negatives with do. Progressive forms 2 grammar For other common structures in which  nouns are used to talk about actions, ►     132.

Have can also be used to mean 'receive' (e.g. I've had a phone call from Emma).

Note American English take a bath/shower/rest/swim/walk.

have an accident / an operation ! a nervous breakdown have difficulty / trouble (in) .. .ing (something) have a try ! ago (BrE)   have a look have a baby (= give birth) have a swim / a walk / a ride / a dance / a game of tennis, etc a row (BrE) / a quarrel / a fight have a talk / a chat / a word with somebody / a conversation / a disagreement / have a good journey / flight / trip, etc have a good time / a bad day / a nice evening / a day off / a holiday (BrE) have a rest / a lie-down (BrE) / a sleep / a dream have a bath (BrE) / a wash / a shave / a shower (BrE) have breakfast / lunch / supper / dinner / tea / coffee / a drink / a meal noun. Common expressions: 'experience' or many other things - the exact meaning depends on the following In expressions like these, have can be the equivalent of 'eat', 'drink', 'enjoy', Have a good time.

I'll have a think (BrE) and let you know what I decide.

Let's have a drink. I'm going to have a bath. (BrE) an informal style.

We   often use have + object to talk about actions and experiences, especially in 1  meaning       and   typical     expressions 23 have:     actions For contractions, ►    337.    For weak forms, ►    315.

1 haven't seen her anywhere, (     I'm not having seen her anywhere.) There are no progressive forms of the auxiliary verb have.

3 progressive         forms not I haven't seen them, (     /  don't have seen them.) not Have you heard the news? (     D&you-kave-ke&rd-... ?) Like all auxiliary verbs, have makes questions and negatives without do.

2 questions       and   negatives grammar •  24  have (got): possession, relationships and other states not The house hasn't got a cellar, (     The house doesn 't have gpt. ..) not    o Have you got a headache? (     D  you have got. ..) Do is not used in questions and negatives with got.

6 have      got:   details tense of have, not the present perfect of get.

Note that have got means exactly the same as have in this case - it is a present Has your sister got a car?  I haven't got your keys.

I've got a new boyfriend. (More natural in speech than I have a new In conversation and informal writing, we  often use the double form have got.

Angela does not have the charm .. . (AmE/BrE) - Angela has not the charm of her older sisters, (formal BrE only) Do you have an appointment? (AmE/BrE) - Have you an appointment? (formal BrE only) They  are not normally used in American English.

uncommon (except in a few fixed expressions like I haven't the faintest idea).

common in older English. In modern English they are rather formal and Short question and negative forms (e.g. have you ... ?, she has not) were 4 shorter      question       and   negative       forms:    have      you     ...  ?; she    has    not Her parents did not have very much  money.

Does the house have a garden?

commonly formed with do for these meanings.

In American English and modern British English, questions and negatives are 3  questions       and   negatives       with    do noi Do you have a headache? (    At&yctudmviHgadieatiat.lie?) not She has three brothers, (     She4s4tavmg-tkfee-brotkers.) Progressive forms of have are not used for these meanings.
